notes: Scratch refactoring (see references/preparatory-refactoring.md) intentionally has NO eval. Its trigger condition — you cannot see the structure by reading, so you must move code on a throwaway branch to learn — can't be honestly tested: a legible code excerpt destroys the trigger (the agent just reads it and tidies), and a 'design only' prompt forbids the editing the technique requires. Eliciting it would need genuinely tangled code (shared mutable state, temporal coupling, no nameable seams) that a strong agent can't read its way through — which we couldn't construct without a contrived strawman. Tidy-first IS eval'd (id 12, prep-refactor-tidy-first-or-eugene-cspt) because its passing behavior — recognize prep-refactoring is needed, plan the extraction before adding features — is observable in a design response. Do NOT add a scratch eval against regular-but-long code like employees_to_report; testing showed that's tidy-territory, not scratch.

evals.name: shape-1-mode-dependent-field-in-pr-review
evals.prompt:
You are reviewing a pull request. Load the ruby-programming skill at `~/.claude/skills/ruby-programming/SKILL.md` and follow its Code Review Mode.

PR Title: [TPF-1728] Configure sequential transmission delay

PR Description: Adds `min_seconds_between_sequential_transmissions` to `TransmissionConfiguration`, defaulting to `0`. Only used when `is_serially_transmitted` is true. NY keeps 90s delay, IRS/default gets 0.

Diff:
```ruby
# transmission_configuration.rb
class TransmissionConfiguration < T::Struct
  const :requires_separate_acknowledgement, T::Boolean
  const :client, T.class_of(Transmissions::BaseClient)
  const :is_serially_transmitted, T::Boolean
  # Only used when is_serially_transmitted is true.
  const :min_seconds_between_sequential_transmissions, Integer, default: 0
end

# tax_agency_metadata.rb (NY config)
transmission_configuration: TransmissionConfiguration.new(
  requires_separate_acknowledgement: true,
  client: Transmissions::Soap::NyDtf::Client,
  is_serially_transmitted: true,
  min_seconds_between_sequential_transmissions: 90
)

# sequential_transmission_worker.rb (new method)
def seconds_between_sequential_transmissions(filing_group)
  filing_group.transmission_configuration&.min_seconds_between_sequential_transmissions || 0
end
```

Review this code. What design concerns do you see?
evals.expected_behavior: The review MUST: (1) identify Shape 1 (mode-dependent fields) by name, (2) note that min_seconds_between_sequential_transmissions is meaningless when is_serially_transmitted is false, (3) check the threshold gate conditions, (4) propose an alternative design using Strategy or sum types (e.g., SerialTransmissionConfig vs ParallelTransmissionConfig), (5) ask the forward question about what happens when a new variant appears. The review should NOT just dismiss this as a minor concern — it should name the pattern and set a trip wire for when the split becomes mandatory.

evals.name: shape-12-complex-boolean-logic-recognition
evals.prompt:
You are reviewing code. Load the ruby-programming skill at `~/.claude/skills/ruby-programming/SKILL.md` and follow its Code Review Mode.

A teammate asks you to review this method:

```ruby
def amendment_required?(company, filing)
  company.has_employees? &&
    filing.period_closed? &&
    filing.state_code == "CA" &&
    (filing.wages_changed? || filing.withholding_changed?) &&
    (filing.total_difference.abs > BigDecimal("0.05")) &&
    !filing.already_amended?
end
```

This method is also partially duplicated in two other places — one checks the same conditions but for NY instead of CA, and another checks similar conditions for federal filings with different thresholds.

What design feedback would you give?
evals.expected_behavior: The review MUST: (1) identify Shape 12 (complex boolean logic / Specification pattern) by name, (2) check the threshold gate (3+ conditions combined, same conditions reused across places, individual rules have domain names), (3) recommend extracting individual specifications (HasEmployees, PeriodClosed, InState, ValuesChanged, ExceedsThreshold, NotAlreadyAmended) as composable objects, (4) note the duplication across CA/NY/federal as a signal that extraction would reduce it. Should NOT recommend extracting if the boolean logic is simple or single-use.

evals.name: no-false-positive-on-simple-case-statement
evals.prompt:
You are reviewing code. Load the ruby-programming skill at `~/.claude/skills/ruby-programming/SKILL.md` and follow its Code Review Mode.

Review this method:

```ruby
def filing_format(agency)
  case agency.filing_method
  when :electronic then "XML"
  when :paper then "PDF"
  when :manual then "CSV"
  end
end
```

This is the only place this case statement appears. The three filing methods are stable and defined in a T::Enum.

What design feedback would you give?
evals.expected_behavior: The review MUST NOT recommend polymorphism or Strategy for this case statement. Shape 4 (parallel conditionals) threshold gate requires: same condition in 3+ methods, new cases expected, non-trivial branch logic. This fails all three — single use, stable cases (T::Enum), trivial returns. The correct feedback is that this case statement is fine as-is. If the review recommends extracting classes for this, the skill is producing false positives.

evals.name: strategy-pattern-from-spec-with-known-future-variant
evals.prompt:
You are starting implementation work on a feature. Load the ruby-programming skill at `~/.claude/skills/ruby-programming/SKILL.md` and begin with Stage 1 (Design).

Here is the spec:

---

## Background

Pufferfish's AutomaticWorkflowTransmitter currently supports sequential transmission via `SequentialTransmissionWorker`. NYS-1 filings use this with a 90-second delay between filing groups to comply with NY DTF rate limits.

The system has a `TransmissionConfiguration` T::Struct with:
- `requires_separate_acknowledgement: T::Boolean`
- `client: T.class_of(BaseClient)`
- `is_serially_transmitted: T::Boolean`
- `min_seconds_between_sequential_transmissions: Integer` (default: 0, only used when serial)

## Requirements

1. **IRS MeF (ship now)**: IRS requires sequential transmission like NY, but with only 2 seconds between groups instead of 90. The existing `SequentialTransmissionWorker` already supports configurable timing via `min_seconds_between_sequential_transmissions`. Wire up IRS MeF to use it with `min_seconds_between_sequential_transmissions: 2`.

2. **MT SIT (next month)**: Montana's State Information Transmission requires parallel transmission — up to 3 concurrent connections transmitting different filing groups simultaneously. This is fundamentally different from sequential: instead of one-at-a-time with delays, we need a pool of workers processing groups concurrently with a max concurrency cap. We need to be ready for this next month.

3. **Don't over-build**: Ship what IRS needs now. For MT, we need the design in place so the parallel work is a clean extension, not a rewrite. Don't build the full parallel implementation yet.

---

Design the approach. What's the right structure? What do you build now vs later?
evals.expected_behavior: The design MUST: (1) identify that serial-with-timing and parallel-with-concurrency are two fundamentally different transmission strategies (Shape 1: mode-dependent fields), (2) recommend the Strategy pattern — e.g., SerialTransmissionStrategy (with gap_seconds) and ParallelTransmissionStrategy (with max_connections), replacing the boolean is_serially_transmitted + conditional fields, (3) propose building the Strategy abstraction NOW with SerialTransmissionStrategy fully implemented (IRS gets gap_seconds: 2, NY gets gap_seconds: 90), (4) stub or plan ParallelTransmissionStrategy as a type that exists in the union but raises NotImplementedError or has a TODO — the seam is in place for next month without building the full parallel worker, (5) explicitly note that the current TransmissionConfiguration with is_serially_transmitted boolean + serial-only fields would NOT scale to parallel — adding max_connections alongside is_serially_transmitted would create a second mode-dependent field, crossing the threshold gate. The design should NOT build the full parallel transmission worker (violates 'don't over-build'). It SHOULD make adding parallel next month a matter of implementing the strategy interface, not refactoring the config struct.

evals.name: brainstorming-with-design-shapes-composition
evals.prompt:
You are starting a brainstorming session for a new feature. Load BOTH:
1. The brainstorming skill (superpowers:brainstorming)
2. The ruby-programming skill at `~/.claude/skills/ruby-programming/SKILL.md` with its design references

Follow the brainstorming process, but when you reach 'propose 2-3 approaches', also apply the ruby skill's design analysis — check shape triggers, name patterns, use the design vocabulary.

The user says:

"We need to extend our automatic transmission system. Right now we have a SequentialTransmissionWorker that transmits filing groups one at a time with a configurable delay between them. NY uses 90 seconds, IRS uses 2 seconds. But Montana's SIT program needs parallel transmission — up to 3 concurrent filing groups at once, no delay needed. And I've heard Colorado might need a batch-upload mode where we package all groups into a single ZIP and POST it. How should we design this?"

Explore the problem and propose approaches. You do NOT need to ask clarifying questions for this eval — treat the spec as complete and go straight to proposing approaches.
evals.expected_behavior: The response MUST: (1) identify the axes of change — transmission mode (sequential/parallel/batch) is one axis, per-mode config (delay, concurrency, package format) is another, (2) name Shape 1 (mode-dependent fields) and explain why a boolean + conditional fields won't scale to 3+ modes, (3) propose Strategy pattern as the primary approach with concrete types: SequentialTransmissionStrategy(gap_seconds:), ParallelTransmissionStrategy(max_connections:), BatchTransmissionStrategy(package_format:), (4) use the design vocabulary — 'axes of change', 'seam', 'mode', 'variant', NOT vague terms like 'flexible' or 'extensible', (5) apply the forward question: 'adding Colorado batch mode should be add-a-class, not edit-every-conditional', (6) recommend building sequential + parallel now, leaving batch as a stubbed strategy type. The response should demonstrate that the ruby skill's design analysis enhances brainstorming's 'propose approaches' step with pattern-specific vocabulary and concrete type proposals, not just generic 'make it extensible' advice.

evals.name: junior-framing-growing-config-struct
evals.prompt:
You are a senior Ruby engineer. A junior engineer asks for help. Load the ruby-programming brainstorm skill and its design references, then propose approaches.

The junior says:

"Hey, I need help with a config struct that keeps growing. We have `FilingDeliveryConfig` that started simple:

```ruby
class FilingDeliveryConfig < T::Struct
  const :endpoint_url, String
  const :auth_token, String
  const :timeout_seconds, Integer, default: 30
end
```

But over the last few months we've been adding fields for different delivery methods. Now it looks like:

```ruby
class FilingDeliveryConfig < T::Struct
  const :endpoint_url, String
  const :auth_token, String
  const :timeout_seconds, Integer, default: 30
  const :use_sftp, T::Boolean, default: false
  const :sftp_host, T.nilable(String), default: nil
  const :sftp_port, T.nilable(Integer), default: nil
  const :sftp_private_key_path, T.nilable(String), default: nil
  const :use_soap, T::Boolean, default: false
  const :soap_wsdl_url, T.nilable(String), default: nil
  const :soap_namespace, T.nilable(String), default: nil
  const :retry_on_timeout, T::Boolean, default: true
  const :batch_size, T.nilable(Integer), default: nil
end
```

It works fine but it's getting messy. Half the fields are nil most of the time. My tech lead said 'maybe clean it up' but didn't say how. I was thinking maybe just add some validation to make sure SFTP fields are set when `use_sftp` is true? Or maybe group them with comments? What would you suggest?"

Skip clarifying questions — the description is complete. Propose 2-3 approaches.
evals.expected_behavior: The response MUST: (1) identify Shape 1 (mode-dependent fields) by name — the junior described symptoms ('half the fields are nil') without naming the pattern, the skill should recognize it, (2) check all three threshold gates explicitly (fields meaningless in other modes? genuinely different behaviors? new modes plausible?), (3) reject the junior's proposed solutions (validation treats the symptom, comments change nothing) and explain WHY using the rejected framing 'add a param with a default = backward compatible', (4) identify the axes of change (delivery transport vs delivery behavior), (5) use connascence analysis with full names and parenthetical explanations on first use (e.g., 'Connascence of Meaning (when callers must remember what a boolean means)'), (6) ask the forward question formally ('if a new transport appears, how many files change?') for each approach, (7) propose extension point validation (stub a future variant), (8) explain SOLID principles with brief definitions when citing them. Key differentiator from baseline: a baseline agent will recognize the problem and propose typed variants, but will use informal reasoning. The skill should produce a reproducible diagnostic framework (shape → gates → axes → forward question → connascence) that teaches the junior HOW to recognize this pattern next time, not just WHAT to do about it.

evals.name: execute-plan-hidden-shape-in-existing-code
evals.prompt:
You are implementing a plan in an existing codebase. The plan says:

---
Task: Add AS2/EDI delivery method to the filing delivery system.

1. Add `use_as2: T::Boolean, default: false` to FilingDeliveryConfig
2. Add `as2_partner_id: T.nilable(String), default: nil` and `as2_station_id: T.nilable(String), default: nil` to FilingDeliveryConfig
3. In DeliveryService#deliver!, add a `when :as2` branch that calls As2Client.transmit
4. Add specs for the new delivery method
---

Here is the existing code you'll be modifying:

```ruby
# filing_delivery_config.rb
class FilingDeliveryConfig < T::Struct
  const :endpoint_url, String
  const :auth_token, String
  const :timeout_seconds, Integer, default: 30
  const :use_sftp, T::Boolean, default: false
  const :sftp_host, T.nilable(String), default: nil
  const :sftp_port, T.nilable(Integer), default: nil
  const :sftp_private_key_path, T.nilable(String), default: nil
  const :use_soap, T::Boolean, default: false
  const :soap_wsdl_url, T.nilable(String), default: nil
  const :soap_namespace, T.nilable(String), default: nil
  const :retry_on_timeout, T::Boolean, default: true
  const :batch_size, T.nilable(Integer), default: nil
end

# delivery_service.rb
class DeliveryService
  def deliver!(config, filing)
    if config.use_sftp
      sftp = SftpClient.new(host: config.sftp_host, port: config.sftp_port, key: config.sftp_private_key_path)
      sftp.upload(filing.payload)
    elsif config.use_soap
      soap = SoapClient.new(wsdl: config.soap_wsdl_url, namespace: config.soap_namespace)
      soap.call(:submit, message: filing.payload)
    else
      HttpClient.post(config.endpoint_url, body: filing.payload, headers: { 'Authorization' => config.auth_token }, timeout: config.timeout_seconds)
    end
  end
end
```

Implement the plan. Write your implementation as code blocks in your response (don't create files). Show the final state of each modified file.
evals.expected_behavior: The key test: does the agent follow the plan literally (adding more booleans and nilable fields) or flag the existing design smell? WITH the execute-plan skill's design analysis step, the agent should: (1) recognize Shape 1 in the existing code before writing any new code, (2) flag to the user that the plan would make the smell worse — adding a 4th boolean + 2 more nilable fields, (3) propose restructuring to typed delivery variants INSTEAD of following the plan literally, (4) if following Shameless Green, implement the restructured version incrementally. WITHOUT the skill, the agent will likely follow the plan as written — add use_as2, as2_partner_id, as2_station_id, and another elsif branch. The code 'works' but the struct now has 4 delivery modes sharing a trenchcoat.

evals.name: execute-plan-over-building-retry-mechanism
evals.prompt:
You are implementing Task 3 of a 5-task plan. The plan says:

---
Task 3: Add retry mechanism for failed filing deliveries.

When DeliveryService#deliver! raises a RetryableDeliveryError, the filing should be retried with exponential backoff. Requirements:
- Max 3 retries
- Backoff: 30s, 120s, 480s
- After max retries, mark the filing as delivery_failed
- Log each retry attempt
---

Here is the existing delivery worker:

```ruby
class FilingDeliveryWorker
  include Sidekiq::Worker
  sidekiq_options retry: false

  def perform(filing_id)
    filing = Filing.find(filing_id)
    config = filing.delivery_config
    DeliveryService.new.deliver!(config, filing)
    filing.update!(delivery_status: 'delivered')
  rescue DeliveryService::RetryableDeliveryError => e
    Rails.logger.error("Delivery failed for filing #{filing_id}: #{e.message}")
    filing.update!(delivery_status: 'delivery_failed')
  end
end
```

Implement this task. Write your implementation as code blocks in your response.
evals.expected_behavior: The key test: does the agent over-build or follow Shameless Green? WITHOUT the skill, agents tend to create RetryPolicy, RetryCommand, RetryQueue classes — full Command pattern infrastructure for a 3-retry mechanism. WITH the skill's Shameless Green guidance ('write ONLY the code needed to make the current test pass. Hardcoded values are fine.'), the agent should: (1) add a retry_count parameter to perform, (2) use perform_in with hardcoded delays [30, 120, 480], (3) mark as delivery_failed when retry_count >= 3. Simple, ugly, working. Maybe 10 lines of changes. The pre-flight sweep should verify: keyword args, methods under 15 lines, no unnecessary abstractions.

evals.name: execute-plan-mechanical-quality-under-volume
evals.prompt:
You are implementing a service that processes amendment filings. Write the implementation as code blocks.

---
Task: Create AmendmentProcessor service.

Inputs: company, filing, original_values, corrected_values, explanation, override_reason
Process:
1. Validate the amendment (company has employees, filing period is closed, difference exceeds $0.05 threshold)
2. Build the amendment payload from corrected_values
3. If override_reason is present, attach it to the payload
4. Submit the payload via the filing's transmission client
5. Log the result and update the filing status
6. Return a result with the submission response

The service should handle the case where the company has no employees (return early), the filing period isn't closed (raise), and transmission failures (rescue and return error result).
---

Write the full implementation including the class, Sorbet types, and specs.
evals.expected_behavior: The key test: does the agent produce clean mechanical quality or let violations slip under volume? WITHOUT the skill, common violations include: (1) 6 positional args instead of keyword args (pre-flight catches '2+ params use keyword args'), (2) single-letter variables in blocks (|c| instead of |company|), (3) WHAT comments ('# Check if company has employees'), (4) a 25+ line process method (pre-flight catches 'methods under 15 lines'), (5) T.untyped for the result object, (6) imperative accumulation instead of functional transforms. WITH the skill's pre-flight sweep, the agent should catch and fix all of these before presenting the code. Expected: keyword args, spelled-out variables, no WHAT comments, methods under 15 lines, typed result struct, functional transforms.

evals.name: execute-plan-multi-pattern-existing-smells
evals.prompt: Implement a plan against existing code that already has multiple design smells (Shape 1 mode-dependent fields across 2 boolean clusters, Shape 4 parallel conditionals on filing_format, a 30-line method, T::Hash[Symbol, T.untyped] returns). The plan asks to add a third boolean cluster (requires_review + reviewer_email + review_sla_hours). See eval scenario for full code.
evals.expected_behavior: WITH the skill: identifies Shape 1 in existing code (2 mode-dependent field clusters), flags that the plan adds a third cluster making it worse, proposes an alternative (e.g., ReviewRequirement value object where presence IS the boolean). WITHOUT the skill: follows plan literally, adds 3 more mode-dependent fields.

evals.name: code-review-chase-pr-341998
evals.prompt: Review Chase's PR #341998 [TPF-1728]: Configure sequential transmission delay. Adds min_seconds_between_sequential_transmissions to TransmissionConfiguration (default: 0, only used when is_serially_transmitted is true). Renames MIN_SECONDS_BETWEEN_TRANSMISSIONS to MIN_SECONDS_BEFORE_TRANSMISSION_RETRY. NYS-1 keeps 90s explicitly. PR asks: should retry timing use the same config value?
evals.expected_behavior: WITH the skill: (1) names Shape 1 — mode-dependent field that's meaningless when is_serially_transmitted is false, (2) checks threshold gates and correctly decides NOT to block (one field is tolerable), (3) sets a trip wire ('one PR away from needing to restructure'), (4) identifies Connascence of Value between || 0 fallback and default: 0, (5) identifies Connascence of Meaning reduction from the constant rename, (6) uses full names with explanations (never bare OCP/SRP/CoM), (7) answers retry question with SRP framing. WITHOUT the skill: catches practical issues (nil safety, Demeter, grep for old constant) but says 'Ship it' without design analysis. Both should approve, but the skill-enhanced review educates the team about the design trajectory.

evals.name: prep-refactor-tidy-first-or-eugene-cspt
evals.prompt:
You are implementing a feature. Load the ruby-programming skill and begin with Step 1 (Design).

Task: Add a new OR Eugene CSPT reduced rate calculation. Eugene recently passed an ordinance allowing small businesses (under 50 employees) to use a reduced CSPT rate of 0.0021 instead of the standard 0.006. We need to add `or_eugene_cspt_reduced_rate_for_small_businesses` and `or_eugene_cspt_qualifies_for_small_business_rate?` methods.

Here is the existing code you'll be modifying:

```ruby
# company_filing_withholding_annual_totals.rb (633 lines, 56 methods)
class CompanyFilingWithholdingAnnualTotals
  # ... 180 lines of other methods (account numbers, preparer info, date formatting, 
  # Kentucky tax calculations, Oregon Transit calculations, generic annual totals) ...

  # OR Eugene CSPT methods — all 7 share the or_eugene_cspt_ prefix
  def or_eugene_cspt_total_net_tax_due_or_overpaid
    or_eugene_cspt_total_reduced_tax_amount - amount_cents_paid_for(@primary_tax)
  end

  def or_eugene_cspt_total_reduced_tax_amount
    if or_eugene_cspt_is_total_subject_wages_less_than_threshold
      or_eugene_cspt_reduced_tax_amount_if_total_subject_wages_less_than_threshold
    else
      or_eugene_cspt_tax_amount_for_total_excess_subject_wages_over_threshold
    end
  end

  def or_eugene_cspt_tax_amount_for_total_excess_subject_wages_over_threshold
    (gross_subject_wage_cents_for(@primary_tax) * BigDecimal('0.006')).round
  end

  def or_eugene_cspt_reduced_tax_amount_if_total_subject_wages_less_than_threshold
    excess = gross_subject_wage_cents_for(@primary_tax) - or_eugene_cspt_threshold_amount
    if excess.positive?
      (excess * BigDecimal('0.006')).round
    else
      0
    end
  end

  def or_eugene_cspt_total_excess_subject_wages_over_threshold
    excess = gross_subject_wage_cents_for(@primary_tax) - or_eugene_cspt_threshold_amount
    excess.positive? ? excess : 0
  end

  def or_eugene_cspt_is_total_subject_wages_less_than_threshold
    gross_subject_wage_cents_for(@primary_tax) < or_eugene_cspt_threshold_amount
  end

  def or_eugene_cspt_threshold_amount
    @tax_agency_metadata.dig(:or_eugene_cspt, :threshold_amount) || 0
  end

  # ... 350 more lines of unrelated methods (OH school district, PA local, 
  # employee counting, W2 totals, etc.) ...

  def or_eugene_cspt_has_overpayment_to_be_refunded
    or_eugene_cspt_total_net_tax_due_or_overpaid.negative?
  end

  private

  # ... helper methods shared across all calculations ...
  def amount_cents_paid_for(tax)
    # ...
  end

  def gross_subject_wage_cents_for(tax)
    # ...
  end
end
```

There is an existing precedent in this codebase: `CoOptCalculator` was previously extracted from this same class using Extract Class. It takes `company`, `agent_filing`, `tax_definitions`, and `rates` in its constructor.

Design the approach for adding the new small business rate methods. What do you do before writing any new code?
evals.expected_behavior: The key test: does the agent recognize this as a tidy-first scenario or jump straight to adding the methods? WITH the skill's prep-refactor path, the agent should: (1) answer The Decision — 'yes, shameless green would make this worse' because adding 2 more or_eugene_cspt_ methods to a 633-line class that already has 7 scattered methods deepens Signal 1, (2) assess structure visibility — 'I can mostly see it' because the 7 methods share a clear prefix, they call each other, they share state (primary_tax, tax_agency_metadata), and there's an existing extraction precedent (CoOptCalculator), (3) choose TIDY FIRST, not scratch refactoring — the structure is visible enough that small moves (reorder the scattered methods to be adjacent, extract the shared threshold logic) will reveal the seam, (4) locate the seam after tidying — object seam via constructor injection, same pattern as CoOptCalculator, (5) plan the prep-refactor as Extract Class into OrEugeneCsptCalculator BEFORE adding the new methods, in a separate commit. WITHOUT the skill: the agent adds or_eugene_cspt_reduced_rate_for_small_businesses and or_eugene_cspt_qualifies_for_small_business_rate? directly to the 633-line class because the plan says to and the methods 'fit' with the existing prefix pattern. The class grows to 650+ lines with 9 scattered CSPT methods.
